text:
The border deaths of migrants whose lives have been irregularized constitute a strange and opaque site in the geopolitics of migration. While deaths of migrants often serve as a crisis device or act of fate in news reports, the migrants themselves are erased from the official government statistics, as bodies are rarely recorded as migrant when they are autopsied. In the absence of official data, bodies are buried by cemetery administrators and mourned by activists, for their own reasons: to bury the bodies properly, and to remember the lives of those who have been lost in the course of trying to reach relative safety. The authorities only start to take an interest in border deaths when pressure from media and other citizens mounts.

Soundtracks of Migration introduces readers to the multiple records created by various stakeholders to document the deaths of migrants en route and in foreign lands. It engages specifically with the soundtracks of these deaths as they were recorded by the UNITED (UNITE for Intercultural Action) campaign of deaths in detention by policy makers, and with the Soundtracks of deaths at the borders of Europe as documented by the Missing Migrants Project of the IOM (International Organization of Migration). The recording of these deaths by UNITED in the 1990ies turned the deaths of migrants into a political currency, casting deaths in the borderlands as ‘death by policy’ to signify a specific form of structural violence enacted by the state. The IOM records, started after the major “Lampedusa-tragedy” in 2013, are framing migrants deaths as a predictable side effect of migration of humanitarian character, thus “humanitarianizing” deaths as inevitable collateral damage caused “along the way of migration”, and at the same time “naturalizing” it by focusing less on state responsibility and rather on the “complexity” of migration by pointing at various actors along a complex “migration route”. The IOM thus follows a trend in the emerging “migration industry” a place where care and policing blend together.

Data-driven advocacy is not a “new” tool for advocacy work, but it can certainly be “game-changing”. We use the examples of wartime victims in Croatia, where numbers of victims can still be a highly politicised issue, as well as an example of the border deaths in Croatia – a topic that does not provoke much opposition at all. In the article, we show how the different numbers on victims of the wartime conflicts “swirl around in an amorphous mass” and in the end do not lead to a tangible influence on politics. For example, an insignificant increase in asylum applications can lead to the reintroduction of control measures at Schengen borders, while a constant growth in border deaths does not seem to raise sufficient red flags for authorities to take action.

The Documenting Border Deaths project and the spectacle of violence This article responds to the critique from feminist, decolonial and abolitionist scholarship on documentation with respect to the border regime. Is documentation a tool to challenge and undermine the border regime, or is it part of the spectacle of violence that perpetuates the very dynamics it seeks to disrupt? Does documenting border deaths and vulnerability reduce victims to numbers and data points, benefiting those documenting the deaths and reinforcing existing power relations? Through a critical discourse analysis of the International Organization for Migration’s (IOM) communication strategy and reports in relation to their border deaths documentation project, this article argues that NGOs’ and IGOs’ practice of documenting deaths and deaths locations can perpetuate dehumanising and discriminatory border regime policies. However, the article also highlights the potential of vernacular forms of documentation created by survivors, families and loved ones of those who have died at the border and have been rendered illegible and irrelevant in official accounts and statistics. Based on affective solidarity, a term introduced by the political theorist Clare Hemmings, which is rooted in grief, mourning, resentment and the struggle to acknowledge others’ pain and therefore one’s own, these grassroots forms of documentation represent a possibility to move beyond documentation as a tool of control and rather create a sense of discontent for societal structures that produce deaths at the border and render these deaths as utterly grievable.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
